name: Hastamalaka
dates_notes: Known only from sectarian tradition as one of Śaṅkara's four chief disciples; one short hymn survives.
core_thesis: Hastāmalaka, traditionally one of Śaṅkara's four chief disciples, is known almost exclusively through a single brief *stotra*. His position, as articulated in the hymn, is uncompromising *nirupādhika-ātmā-jñāna*: the *ātman* is the eternal self-luminous (*svayam-prakāśa*) witness-consciousness (*sākṣin*) that is identical with *brahman*, intrinsically free of the body-mind aggregate's transformations, and is what is referred to as *aham* when the *aham*-cognition is understood at its true *paramārtha* depth rather than at the level of *adhyāsa*. The hymn's repeated refrain — *nityopalabdhi-svarūpo 'ham ātmā* ('I am the self whose nature is constant cognizance') — is a compact statement of the *advaita* identity-thesis from the first-person *anubhava*-side. As a doctrinal reconstruction of Hastāmalaka the figure beyond what the hymn says, the historical evidence is essentially nil: the *Maṭha* tradition treats him as the founder of one of the four cardinal *āmnāya* lineages, but no biographical or doctrinal corpus survives.

engaged_works.title: Hastamalaka-Stotra
engaged_works.ascription_notes: Traditionally ascribed; the hymn is universally received in the Advaita maṭha tradition though independent academic verification is limited.
engaged_works.summary: Short *stotra* of twelve verses (some recensions add a *phala-śruti*), traditionally framed by a narrative in which the boy Hastāmalaka, asked by Śaṅkara *ko bhavān* ('who are you?'), responds in spontaneous verse by identifying himself as the witness-consciousness *ātman*, not the body-mind. Each verse follows the same structural pattern: a description of mental and bodily activities — the operations of *manas*, *buddhi*, *indriyas*, the alternation of *jāgrat*–*svapna*–*suṣupti*, the cycle of *karma* — followed by a refrain affirming the speaker's identity with the unchanging self-luminous *ātman* who illuminates all of these without being any of them. The hymn is recited liturgically in Advaita *maṭha* contexts and is part of the standard *prakaraṇa* corpus alongside short Śaṅkara-ascribed *stotras*. Traditionally ascribed; the hymn is universally received in the Advaita *maṭha* tradition though independent academic verification is limited and the historical Hastāmalaka cannot be securely identified beyond the tradition's claim.
